event: Glorification of Saint Martha ascending to heaven
symbols: upward gaze — ecstatic contemplation and yearning for the divine presence
angels bearing the saint aloft — divine honor and heavenly reception of the faithful departed
swirling clouds — the threshold between earthly and heavenly realms
putti — celestial joy and innocence attending the glorified saint
golden light from above — the radiance of God's glory and the beatific vision
outstretched arms — surrender to divine will and joyful embrace of heavenly glory
blue mantle — heavenly identity and spiritual transformation from earthly servant to glorified saint
description: A circular tondo composition depicting a woman in a blue mantle and golden-brown garment being carried upward through billowing clouds by multiple angels and putti. The central female figure gazes ecstatically upward with arms outstretched, her body dynamically twisting in an ascending spiral. Large winged angels support her on either side, while numerous small putti surround her below and at the edges. The scene is suffused with warm golden light emanating from the upper portion. The painting is set within a round gilded frame mounted on a dark panel with the inscription 'LEGATO MERLI M.D.
theological_description: Gaulli's 'Gloria di Santa Marta' presents the heavenly glorification of Martha of Bethany, transforming the humble servant of Luke 10:38-42 into a triumphant saint received into celestial glory. Martha, who served Christ in her home and declared the confession 'I believe that you are the Christ, the Son of God' (John 11:27) — one of the most profound Christological declarations in the Gospels — is here shown receiving the reward promised to the faithful. The ascending movement surrounded by angels reflects the eschatological promise that those who serve the Lord will be honored by the Father (John 12:26). Martha's heavenly ecstasy represents the theological reversal celebrated in the Magnificat: the humble are exalted (Luke 1:52). The woman who was 'anxious and troubled about many things' in her earthly service now enters the one thing needful — the unmediated presence of God. The angelic escort echoes the parable of Lazarus, where angels carry the righteous to Abraham's bosom (Luke 16:22), extending this honor to Lazarus's own sister. The baroque dynamism of the composition expresses the theology of glorification found in Romans 8:30 — those whom God justified, he also glorified.

scripture_refs.reason: Martha's great confession of Christ as the Son of God is the theological foundation for her glorification
Martha's identity as the one who served the Lord in her home — her earthly service now rewarded with heavenly glory
Where Christ is, there his servant will be also — Martha who served is now honored in the Father's presence
Angels carrying the righteous to paradise parallels the angelic escort bearing Martha heavenward
The glorification of the justified — Martha's ascent visually expresses the final stage of the golden chain of salvation
The humble servant exalted — the Magnificat's reversal made manifest in Martha's heavenly triumph
